title: Repentance and Prayer for Righteousness
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander
source_files: Repentance and Prayer for Righteousness - Elder Scott Hollander - Psalm 5_1-12.txt

outline.title: I. Come with Confidence Before the Throne of God (Psalm 5:1-3)
subpoints: Application 1: Plead for an audience with Yahweh — David's three-fold intensifying plea (give ear, consider, give heed)
David addresses Yahweh as his King and his God, combining sovereignty and deity
Application 2: Come confidently to the throne of grace — David asserts God does hear his morning prayer
David directs or sets in order his prayer to God, presenting his case
He looks up — in confidence, watchfulness for God's answer, and readiness to follow in righteousness

outline.title: II. Pursue Righteousness on Account of the Covenantal Love of God (Psalm 5:4-9)
subpoints: Application 3: Put wickedness far from you — God does not take pleasure in wickedness nor dwell with evil
The unrepentant sinner faces God's just wrath — God hates the wicked, not merely the sin abstractly
Application 4: Humble yourself in worship — David comes only by the multitude of Yahweh's mercy (hesed)
Salvation is by grace through faith, not our own righteousness (Ephesians 2:8-10)
Application 5: Lean on the Word of the Lord — David prays for God to lead him in righteousness
Application 6: Avoid the sins of deceit and injustice — false witness, flattery, and corruption described

outline.title: III. Side with the Righteousness of God and Rejoice in Him (Psalm 5:10-12)
subpoints: Application 7: Pray for justice — imprecatory prayer invoking ironic justice on the wicked (like Haman in Esther, Daniel's accusers)
Application 8: Believe, rejoice, and trust in God — those who trust are defended and surrounded with favor as a shield
The first question of the Shorter Catechism: man's chief end is to glorify God and enjoy Him forever
Yahweh defends those who love and trust in Him — Psalm 84:11 supports this
Call to repentance for all who have not bowed to Christ in faith

summary:
Elder Scott Hollander preaches from Psalm 5, a morning psalm that David wrote to be accompanied by flutes — the only such psalm in the Psalter. He presents the sermon title 'Repentance and Prayer for Righteousness' and organizes the psalm around three main responses for the modern Christian: keeping a short record of accounts regarding sin, pursuing righteousness on account of God's covenantal loyal love, and siding with God's righteousness by rejoicing in Him. The sermon begins with an examination of Hebrew poetic structure, noting the Psalm's pattern of request, reason, and contrast that makes it easy to follow in English translation.

Hollander argues that the Psalm's so-called 'proof texts' about God hating the wicked should be read in context: David's citation of God's hatred for sin is not merely doctrinal but is his personal motivation for repentance, worship, and obedience. David comes before God not on the basis of his own goodness but only through the multitude of Yahweh's covenantal mercy — the same grace believers have in Christ Jesus. The sermon closes with eight practical applications drawn from the psalm, urging believers to plead for an audience with God, come confidently to the throne of grace, put wickedness far from them, humble themselves in worship, lean on the Word, avoid deceit and injustice, pray for justice, and rejoice in the protection of the Lord. Listeners who have not yet bowed to Christ are directly called to repentance.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Psalm 5

If you're able, please remain standing for a moment longer. We're going to be reading from the word of the Lord in Psalm chapter 5.

To the chief musician with flutes, a Psalm of David. Give ear to my words, O Lord, consider my meditation. Give heed to the voice of my cry, my King and my God, for to You I will pray. My voice You shall hear in the morning, O Lord; in the morning I will direct it to You, and I will look up. For You are not a God who takes pleasure in wickedness, nor shall evil dwell with You. The boastful shall not stand in Your sight; You hate all workers of iniquity. You shall destroy those who speak falsehood; the Lord abhors the bloodthirsty and deceitful man. But as for me, I will come into Your house in the multitude of Your mercy; in fear of You I will worship toward Your holy temple. Lead me, O Lord, in Your righteousness because of my enemies; make Your way straight before my face. For there is no faithfulness in their mouth; their inward part is destruction, their throat is an open tomb, they flatter with their tongue. Pronounce them guilty, O God; let them fall by their own counsels, cast them out in the multitude of their transgressions, for they have rebelled against You. But let all those rejoice who put their trust in You; let them ever shout for joy, because You defend them; let those also who love Your name be joyful in You. For You, O Lord, will bless the righteous; with favor You will surround them as with a shield.

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray. Father, we pray to bless the preaching of Your word this morning. We ask in Jesus' name, Amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction

With a quick show of hands, how many of you noticed that our first hymn this morning matched our Scripture reading just a moment ago? Good — that's great.

I can't speculate just how much interaction a diverse congregation like this might have had with Psalm 5 in the past. Likely you've read through it in personal or family devotions. You've worked through it in a Bible study, or even remember details about it from a previous sermon. With a show of hands, how many of you have ever seen it used or used it yourself as a proof text that God hates the wicked? Numbers are fewer, but there's a few. How many of you have ever seen it used as a call to repentance and worship? The numbers are dwindling.

I personally have used Psalm 5 in the past as one of many passages that speaks to God's hatred for the wicked — not just man's sin abstractly, but God's hatred of the unrepentant sinner, the man who refuses to come to Christ in faith. I think the Psalm does that, but I would like for us to see that in context David's citing of God's hatred for sin is in fact his motivation for repentance, obedience, and worship. It is likewise a call for the reader to repentance, worship, and confidence in Yahweh.

## Background and Structure

I want to give you some background information to the Psalm itself, and I want us to consider the overall structure before we get into the practical lessons. Understanding the structure allows us to appreciate the flow of the passage and highlights some aspects of Hebrew poetry.

As is typical, Psalm 5 begins with an introductory section signifying the addressee, the tune or musical instrument, and the author: to the chief musician with flutes, a Psalm of David. Intriguingly, this Psalm is to be accompanied by flutes — this is the only Psalm with this accompaniment. I searched commentaries and thought about the differences between musical instruments, but the meaning is ultimately obscure because this is the only occurrence of the word in the Old Testament. Jewish scholars tend to interpret the word as a type of tune to be played, not a type of instrument at all.

Psalm 4 could be classified as a nighttime Psalm; Psalm 5 is a morning Psalm. I would encourage you to read Psalm 4 this evening as a family before you go to bed tonight. Both Psalms show David seeking an audience with God, a contrast against the unfaithful, and a request for peace and blessing. I was also doing studies this week in Hebrews 4 and found several similarities — so I would encourage you to read Psalm 4 and Hebrews 4 later today.

In considering the structure of Psalm 5, I segmented two major portions: David's plea in verses 1 through 7, and David's prayer in verses 8 through 12. Throughout the Psalm there is a pattern of request, reason, and contrast. The request sections are identifiable by the use of verbal imperatives such as give and consider in verses 1 and 2, and lead and make in verse 8. The reason subsections are recognizable with the marked conjunction for, while the contrast sections are marked with the conjunction but.

## Sermon Title and Three Main Points

My sermon title is Repentance and Prayer for Righteousness. I've broken the Psalm into three responses that we can draw from the text:

1. Keep a short record of accounts regarding your sin.
2. Pursue righteousness on account of the covenantal, loyal love of God.
3. Side with the righteousness of God and rejoice in Him.

## I. Come with Confidence Before the Throne of God (Verses 1-3)

**Application 1: Plead for an audience with Yahweh.**

David writes, give ear to my words, O Lord, consider my meditation, give heed to the voice of my cry, my King and my God.

In these first two verses, David is asking for an audience with these intensifying, commanding pleas to be heard. The formula requests of his prayer come later, after his audience with Yahweh has been established. David's plea is typically poetic in its restructuring of a similar concept: A — hear my words; B — don't just hear me but pay attention; C — listen attentively to my cry.

Phrase A is a simple request: Yahweh, hear my words. Phrase B — consider my meditation — asks for more attention. Don't just listen but pay attention to me. The object in B is not just his words but his meditation — his sigh or groaning. No words are uttered, but David needs Yahweh to pay attention so He can interpret even David's groaning. Have you ever felt the need to pray and not been able to find the words? Paul speaks to this in Romans 8:26: the Spirit makes intercession for us with groanings which cannot be uttered. Finally, in phrase C David repeats his request with new terminology — he is asking Yahweh to listen attentively to the sound of his voice as he utters his cry for help.

Here David states his fealty personally and confidently: Yahweh, you are my King and my God. He is addressing both the authoritative sovereignty and deity of Yahweh. This reminds me of Psalm 121: I will lift up my eyes to the hills. My help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth.

**Application 2: Come confidently to the throne of grace.**

My voice You shall hear in the morning, O Lord; in the morning I will direct it to You and I will look up.

Verse 3 consists of three assertive statements. First, David asserts that God does hear his voice in the morning — David is confident that Yahweh attends to his prayers. Second, David strengthens this by stating he does indeed direct his prayer to God in the morning. The verb can be understood in a legal sense — to present a case, to lay out, to set in rows, to order one's affairs. I translate it as I present my case or I order my affairs before You. Every morning David assesses his own affairs, setting up his case to present before Yahweh — considering the state of his sin, his need for repentance, and his need for direction from God. Third, and I will look up — this small phrase can mean to look up in confidence, to be watchful for what God will do in answer to prayer, or to remain watchful to follow in God's paths of righteousness.

## II. Pursue Righteousness on Account of the Covenantal Love of God (Verses 4-9)

**Application 3: Put wickedness far from you.**

For You are not a God who takes pleasure in wickedness, nor shall evil dwell with You. The boastful shall not stand in Your sight; You hate all workers of iniquity.

Here are the so-called proof-text verses. God does not take pleasure in wickedness, and He does not allow evil to dwell in His presence. The Psalmist demonstrates that God does not make a distinction between hating the sin but loving the sinner — it is the unrepentant sinner that God will cast into hell, not merely the wicked deeds from eternity past. The known justice of God and His holiness instill a fear compelling obedience for the Psalmist.

**Application 4: Humble yourself in worship.**

But as for me, I will come into Your house in the multitude of Your mercy; in fear of You I will worship toward Your holy temple.

In David's contrastive statement, he is not merely demonstrating that he is not like the rebellious. He is not staking his case on the fact that they are evil and he is good. Rather David is humbling himself — it is only because of Yahweh's covenantal loyal love that he has any standing with God at all. Brothers and sisters, this is precisely what we have in Christ. It is not our own righteousness that saves us. But our salvation is by grace through faith, and that not of yourselves; it is the gift of God, not of works, lest anyone should boast. For we are His workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works (Ephesians 2:8-10).

**Application 5: Lean on the Word of the Lord.**

Lead me, O Lord, in Your righteousness because of my enemies; make Your way straight before my face.

David prays that Yahweh would lead him in righteousness. He does not want to be like the wicked whom he identifies as his enemies. He desires that Yahweh continue to lead and guide him — to smooth his path, to prop him up and enable further obedience and service. Believer, this needs to be our prayer as well. We must humbly acknowledge that there is nothing good in us that has drawn God to us. We must seek the wisdom of God and the leading of His Spirit through His Word. As Peter declared, His divine power has given to us all things that pertain to life and godliness. As Paul instructed Timothy, all Scripture is given by inspiration of God and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete.

**Application 6: Avoid the sins of deceit and injustice.**

For there is no faithfulness in their mouth; their inward part is destruction, their throat is an open tomb, they flatter with their tongue.

David explains that there is no faithfulness in the mouth of the wicked — neither faithfulness as submission to Yahweh, nor faithfulness to truth. They lie, bear false witness, flatter, and manipulate. Death is in their throat, and the tomb remains open so that the stench of death readily pours out. David, as a king, hated these wicked men — he wanted no part in that because he knew that Yahweh wanted no part in that.

## III. Side with the Righteousness of God and Rejoice in Him (Verses 10-12)

**Application 7: Pray for justice.**

Pronounce them guilty, O God; let them fall by their own counsels, cast them out in the multitude of their transgressions, for they have rebelled against You.

Although the words of David are an imprecatory prayer, note the restraint. He is asking that their own words of lies and corruption fall back on them — ironic justice. Can you think of other cases of ironic justice like this in Scripture? Haman in the book of Esther erected a post to hang Mordecai upon — he was hanged on his own gallows. In Daniel 6, the wicked administrators who devised a scheme to cast Daniel into the lions' den were themselves devoured by the lions along with their families.

**Application 8: Believe, rejoice, and trust in God.**

But let all those rejoice who put their trust in You; let them ever shout for joy, because You defend them. Let those also who love Your name be joyful in You. For You, O Lord, will bless the righteous; with favor You will surround them as with a shield.

What is the first question and answer of the Shorter Catechism? What is the chief end of man? Man's chief end is to glorify God and to enjoy Him forever. David understood that the righteous believer need merely obey and serve out of the fear of Yahweh's sovereignty and righteous justice. If we truly hope and trust in Yahweh, we may rejoice and shout for joy, because when God is for us, who can be against us? The songs of Korah likewise asserted in Psalm 84:11: For the Lord God is a sun and shield; the Lord will give grace and glory; no good thing will He withhold from those who walk uprightly.

## Conclusion: A Call to Repentance

Not only is this Psalm a call to action for the believer, it is a warning and a call to repentance for any who have not yet bowed to Jesus Christ in faith. There is no good deed that you can do to reconcile your relationship with God. You must humbly bow the knee and take Jesus as the Lord of your life. You must repent of your sins — which means that you must hate them, turn away from them, and cling to the righteousness of God in Christ Jesus. If you have never done this, I pray that you will do that today even before you rise from your seat.

I want to encourage you to read Psalm 4 and Hebrews 4 this evening, and consider the similarities between those two passages and Psalm 5.

Let's close in prayer. Our Father and our God, we are so grateful that You hear our prayers, that You have called us into Your presence, that You have given us our Mediator, the Lord Christ Jesus, and the Holy Spirit who, understanding our hearts, can communicate to You when we don't even have the words to express our needs and our desires. We ask that You would fill us with Your Spirit and enable us for further worship. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.
